task.name: Fresh-context wave handoff
task.objective: Arm a one-shot handoff so the NEXT supervisor wave runs in a clean context: validate the plan file, refuse when another dispatcher owns the window, write `.tmux-cli/fresh-handoff` atomically, print one confirmation line, and STOP. The `tmux-supervisor-cycle.sh` Stop hook consumes the marker after the turn ends and performs `/clear` + `/tmux:supervisor <plan-file>` from outside this context. This command NEVER restarts anything itself.

input.arg: Exactly ONE path to the plan file the fresh instance must run (e.g. `.tmux-cli/research/2026-07-21-14/next-wave-2.md`). Any argument `/tmux:supervisor` accepts is valid; a lone plan `.md` is the primary case. Passed through to the marker VERBATIM — never rewritten, absolutised, or normalised.

output.primary: `.tmux-cli/fresh-handoff` — the one-shot YAML marker (fields: `plan`, optional `self_wave`, optional `cycle`, `requested_by`, `created`) written atomically as the LAST action of the turn.
output.secondary: One stdout line: `FRESH HANDOFF armed → <plan-file> (self_wave=N, cycle=M)`.

requirements.note: Filesystem + Bash only. No tmux-cli MCP tools: this command spawns nothing, sends nothing, and kills nothing. Writing the marker is its entire effect.

llm.mandate: NEVER send `/clear`, `/tmux:supervisor`, or any other key sequence — not via `windows-send`, not via `tmux send-keys`. The Stop hook owns delivery. Sending from here reintroduces the blind queued-input dance this command exists to replace, and would race the hook's own send.

NEVER write the marker before the plan file is verified to exist and be non-empty. The hook CONSUMES the marker before sending (one-shot by construction), so a marker pointing at nothing burns a real restart and logs a dead end. Validate first, arm last.

The marker path and field names are a CROSS-COMPONENT CONTRACT — `.tmux-cli/fresh-handoff` with keys `plan:`, `self_wave:`, `cycle:`, `requested_by:`, `created:`. The Stop hook greps these exact byte sequences. Do not rename, re-case, re-nest, or add fields.
Write ATOMICALLY (temp file in the SAME directory, then `mv` rename). A partially-written marker is parseable by a hook that fires mid-write; a rename is not.

Carry `self_wave`/`cycle` ONLY when you actually know them (a supervisor step-9b handoff). A manual user invocation omits both — a human restart is a fresh authorization and the caps reset, symmetrical with typing `/tmux:supervisor plan.md` by hand. Never invent, guess, or default these to 0.
End the turn immediately after printing. Do not summarise, do not plan the next wave, do not read the plan file's contents — the fresh instance does that with the context you are conserving.

step.title: Validate $ARGUMENTS
step.action: Bind PLAN = `$ARGUMENTS` trimmed of surrounding whitespace.

check.condition: PLAN is empty OR contains more than one whitespace-separated token
check.action: Print exactly: `USAGE: /tmux:supervisor:fresh <plan-file> — exactly one existing, non-empty path required.`

step.action: Verify the file with `test -s "$PLAN"` (exists AND non-empty in one check).

check.action: Print exactly: `USAGE: /tmux:supervisor:fresh <plan-file> — '<PLAN>' does not exist or is empty; refusing to arm a handoff pointing at nothing.`

step.title: Guards — refuse when another dispatcher owns this window
step.action: Check for the daemon and recurring-driver markers relative to the project root.

check.action: Print exactly: `REFUSED: taskvisor-active — the daemon is the sole dispatcher; use the goal flow instead of a fresh handoff.`
check.rule: The Stop hook exits early on this same marker, so an armed handoff here would never fire and would ambush a later, unrelated Stop.

check.action: Print exactly: `REFUSED: recurring-active — the recurring driver owns supervisor dispatch; not arming a fresh handoff.`

step.action: Resolve THIS window's name the same way the hook does, so the two agree by construction: read `$TMUX_WINDOW_UUID` from the environment, find the session whose `TMUX_CLI_PROJECT_PATH` equals the project dir, then match `@window-uuid` across `tmux list-windows -F '#{window_id}|#{window_name}'` to get the window name.

check.condition: $TMUX_WINDOW_UUID is unset OR the resolved window name is not exactly `supervisor`
check.action: Print exactly: `REFUSED: not the 'supervisor' window (resolved: <name-or-unknown>) — the Stop hook only acts there; a marker written here would be stranded.`
check.rule: The hook's window match is exactly `supervisor` (bare, window 0). A goal-namespaced `supervisor-<ns>` window is daemon-owned and is correctly refused here.

step.title: Counter carryover
step.action: If you are a running supervisor performing a step-9b handoff and you KNOW your counters, bind SELF_WAVE and CYCLE to their current values (SELF_WAVE post-incremented per the supervisor's own SELF_WAVE rules). Otherwise — a manual `/tmux:supervisor:fresh` typed by a human — leave BOTH unbound.
step.rule: Unbound means the field is OMITTED from the marker entirely — never emitted as an empty value, `null`, or `0`. The fresh instance starts its counters at 0 when a field is absent, which is the intended cap reset for a human-authorized restart.
When bound, these are the cap-integrity half of the contract: they make `/clear` non-laundering, so the 2-self-generated-wave cap and `max_cycles` hold across any number of fresh handoffs.

step.title: Write the marker atomically
step.action: Ensure `.tmux-cli/` exists, then write to a temp file in THAT SAME directory and rename it over the target — the rename is what makes it atomic, and a same-directory temp guarantees a same-filesystem rename:
step.command:
TMP=".tmux-cli/.fresh-handoff.$$.tmp" { printf 'plan: %s
' "$PLAN" [ -n "${SELF_WAVE:-}" ] && printf 'self_wave: %s
' "$SELF_WAVE" [ -n "${CYCLE:-}" ] && printf 'cycle: %s
' "$CYCLE" printf 'requested_by: %s
' "$REQUESTED_BY" printf 'created: %s
' "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" } > "$TMP" && mv -f "$TMP" ".tmux-cli/fresh-handoff"
step.rule: REQUESTED_BY is `supervisor` for a step-9b handoff, `user` for a manual invocation. It is diagnostic only — the hook restarts identically either way — but it is what makes a stale marker in the log attributable.
`plan:` carries the path EXACTLY as the user/supervisor passed it. The hook resolves it relative to the project dir, the same way the supervisor would have.

check.action: Print `ERROR: could not arm fresh handoff: <error>` and STOP. Do NOT leave a temp file behind (`rm -f "$TMP"`).

step.title: Confirm and STOP
step.action: Print EXACTLY one line, substituting `-` for any counter that was intentionally omitted in step 3:
step.template: FRESH HANDOFF armed → <PLAN> (self_wave=<N or ->, cycle=<M or ->)
step.action: STOP — end the turn immediately. No summary, no follow-up tool calls, no further output.
step.rule: Ending the turn is what FIRES the mechanism: the Stop hook runs on turn end, consumes the marker, and sends `/clear` then `/tmux:supervisor <plan>` after its cancellable countdown. Continuing to work here only delays the handoff and burns the context this command exists to conserve.
To abort an armed restart during the countdown, the operator touches `.tmux-cli/cancel-cycle` — the existing cancel mechanism applies to this branch unchanged.

execution-rules.rule: The marker is the ONLY side effect — no sends, no spawns, no kills, no edits to any other file.
Validate before arming, always: `test -s` on the plan file gates every marker write.
Guards are refusals, not warnings — `taskvisor-active`, `recurring-active`, and a non-`supervisor` window each abort with no marker written.
Counters are carried only when genuinely known; omission is the correct encoding of a human-authorized cap reset.
One marker per turn, written last, atomically, then STOP.
execution-rules.error-reporting: On a genuine harness/infra/spec defect (an MCP tool or `tmux-cli` command that errored after its own retries, a contradictory/under-specified goal spec, or a validator-error-class condition), follow the shared `error-reporting` procedure in `tmux/task-report.xml` — compose + submit autonomously, honoring api.enabled / DEDUP / RECURSION-guard. NEVER on expected work failures (a missing/empty plan file, a guard refusal, an operator-cancelled countdown) or daemon-owned goal-failures.
